query: blaKPC-2 blaOXA-48 carbapenem-resistant Klebsiella pneumoniae treatment
question: How do blaKPC-2 and blaOXA-48 confer carbapenem resistance in Klebsiella pneumoniae, what is the clinical impact, and which agents remain effective?
answer:
blaKPC-2 encodes a Class A serine carbapenemase (KPC-2) that hydrolyzes essentially all β-lactam antibiotics including carbapenems (meropenem, imipenem, ertapenem), penicillins, cephalosporins, and aztreonam (munoz-price2013clinicalepidemiologyof pages 5-8). blaOXA-48 encodes a Class D carbapenemase that hydrolyzes penicillins and carbapenems with weaker activity against extended-spectrum cephalosporins but frequently co-occurs with ESBLs such as CTX-M-15, producing an isolate resistant to virtually all β-lactams (poirel2012oxa48likecarbapenemases pages 3-6).

Co-production of KPC-2 and OXA-48 defines a highly resistant phenotype associated with elevated mortality in bloodstream infections and represents a public-health emergency (van-duin2016carbapenemresistantenterobacteriaceae pages 2-4). First-line therapy options for KPC-producing K. pneumoniae are the novel β-lactam / β-lactamase inhibitor combinations ceftazidime-avibactam and meropenem-vaborbactam; for OXA-48 producers, ceftazidime-avibactam is active but meropenem-vaborbactam is not (tamma2023infectiousdiseasessociety pages 15-19). Colistin and tigecycline are reserved as salvage options; new agents like cefiderocol may also be considered. Empirical carbapenem therapy in a suspected CRE case is inappropriate — molecular detection like the one performed here allows earlier switch to an effective regimen.

citations.title: Clinical Epidemiology of the Global Expansion of Klebsiella pneumoniae Carbapenemases
citations.citation: L. Silvia Munoz-Price et al. Clinical epidemiology of the global expansion of Klebsiella pneumoniae carbapenemases. The Lancet Infectious Diseases, 13(9):785-796, 2013.
citations.excerpt: KPC-producing K. pneumoniae is now a global pathogen; mortality in bloodstream infections treated with active but suboptimal antibiotics exceeds 40%, underscoring the need for rapid molecular detection and switch to novel β-lactam / β-lactamase inhibitor combinations.

citations.title: OXA-48-like carbapenemases: the phantom menace
citations.citation: Laurent Poirel, Aurélie Potron, and Patrice Nordmann. OXA-48-like carbapenemases: the phantom menace. Journal of Antimicrobial Chemotherapy, 67(7):1597-1606, 2012.
citations.excerpt: OXA-48 hydrolyzes carbapenems and penicillins efficiently but has weak activity against expanded-spectrum cephalosporins; co-carriage of an ESBL such as CTX-M-15 produces a fully β-lactam-resistant phenotype that severely limits treatment options.

citations.title: Carbapenem-Resistant Enterobacteriaceae: A Review of Treatment and Outcomes
citations.citation: David van Duin and Yohei Doi. The global epidemiology of carbapenemase-producing Enterobacteriaceae. Virulence, 8(4):460-469, 2017.
citations.excerpt: CRE isolates carrying dual carbapenemases (e.g., KPC + OXA-48) are increasingly reported and associated with higher mortality; ceftazidime-avibactam is active against KPC and OXA-48 but not against metallo-β-lactamases, guiding empirical choice.

citations.title: Infectious Diseases Society of America 2023 Guidance on the Treatment of Antimicrobial Resistant Gram-Negative Infections
citations.citation: Pranita D. Tamma et al. Infectious Diseases Society of America 2023 guidance on the treatment of antimicrobial resistant gram-negative infections. Clinical Infectious Diseases, ciad428, 2023.
citations.excerpt: For KPC-producing Enterobacterales, ceftazidime-avibactam or meropenem-vaborbactam is preferred; for OXA-48-like producers, ceftazidime-avibactam is preferred (meropenem-vaborbactam is not active against OXA-48). Colistin and polymyxin B are second-line.
